title: Pilosella farinoderma
intro: Pilosella farinoderma, also known as False Foxes Tail or a variety of Pilosella, is a flowering plant species within the dandelion family (Asteraceae). It has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat a range of health issues, including digestive problems, inflammation, and respiratory issues, due to its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Pilosella farinoderma include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from this plant are teas, infusions, and tinctures, often used to treat various ailments and to promote overall well-being.
taxonomy: Pilosella farinoderma is classified within the family Asteraceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. The genus Pilosella is part of the tribe Senecioneae. Pilosella farinoderma is native to Europe and has several subspecies. It is an annual or biennial plant that can grow up to a certain height. The classification of Pilosella farinoderma is still a subject of ongoing research and taxonomic studies.
uses: Pilosella farinoderma has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The plant's extracts have been studied for their potential in treating various health conditions, including skin infections and wounds. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of Pilosella farinoderma's medicinal uses. Traditional healers have utilized the plant to create topical treatments and infusions for soothing skin irritations and promoting healing. Further investigation is required to fully understand the plant's potential in modern medicine.

Sesquiterpene lactones are also known to exhibit antioxidant and cytotoxic effects. side_effects: Pilosella farinoderma is considered to be a weed, and it's not well-studied in terms of its effects on human health.
